questions.question: Which of the following statements best describes the Indian Union Cabinet’s role in legislation?
questions.option_1: The Cabinet gives final shape to government bills and prepares the legislative agenda.
questions.option_2: The Cabinet enacts laws without any involvement of any house of the Indian Parliament.
questions.option_3: The Cabinet is the only law-making body in India.
questions.option_4: The Cabinet has judicial authority over legislations surpassing the judiciary.
questions.solution:
Role of Union Cabinet in Legislation

The Union Cabinet is the supreme decision-making body in India. Its primary role in legislation is to give final shape to government bills and prepare the legislative agenda for the Parliament. Most significant bills are drafted by the respective ministries and must receive Cabinet approval before being introduced in either the Lok Sabha or Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: How many cities were initially included under the Smart Cities Mission launched by the Government of India?
questions.solution:
Smart Cities Mission

Launched in June 2015, the Smart Cities Mission initially aimed to develop 100 cities across the country. The goal was to promote cities that provide core infrastructure, a clean and sustainable environment, and a decent quality of life to their citizens through the application of 'Smart' solutions.

questions.question: Following the provisions of the 74ᵗʰ Constitutional Amendment, 1992, which of the following terms is used for urban governance in areas transitioning from rural to urban?
questions.solution:
Nagar Panchayats

The 74th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992, introduced a three-tier structure for urban local bodies. A Nagar Panchayat is established for a transitional area—one that is currently changing from a rural area to an urban area.

questions.question: Which institution has been established by the 74ᵗʰ Constitutional Amendment, 1992, to conduct municipal elections in each State?
questions.solution:
State Election Commission

To ensure free and fair elections to municipal bodies, the 74th Amendment mandated the establishment of a State Election Commission in each state. While the Election Commission of India handles Parliamentary and State Assembly polls, the State Election Commission is solely responsible for municipal and panchayat elections.

questions.question: When were the Municipal Corporations of Calcutta and Bombay established?
questions.solution:
Municipal Corporations (1726)

In 1726, the British East India Company established Municipal Corporations in Calcutta and Bombay (and also Madras) through a Royal Charter. This was one of the earliest formal steps toward urban local governance in colonial India.

questions.question: What major power did the 74ᵗʰ Amendment Act, 1992, provide to Urban Local Bodies?
questions.solution:
Constitutional Status for ULBs

The most significant impact of the 74th Amendment Act, 1992, was that it provided a constitutional outline for urban governance. It added Part IX-A and the 12th Schedule to the Constitution, making it mandatory for states to hold regular elections and devolve specific powers to Urban Local Bodies (ULBs).

questions.question: Which of the following changes did the Waqf (Amendment) Act, 2025 make?
questions.solution:
Waqf (Amendment) Act, 2025

The Waqf (Amendment) Act, 2025, introduced several reforms to the management of Waqf properties. A key highlight was increasing inclusivity by mandating the representation of at least two Muslim women on both the Central Waqf Council and the State Waqf Boards.

questions.question: Which of the following is INCORRECT based on the Supreme Court’s ruling in 'Samtola Devi vs The State Of Uttar Pradesh', on 27 March 2025, regarding the ‘Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act, 2007’?
questions.option_1: Eviction of a child or relative is mandatory in every case of neglect under the Act.
questions.option_2: Senior citizens can file for maintenance even after transferring their property to their legal heirs.
questions.option_3: The Act does not provide any provision for the eviction of a child or relative.
questions.option_4: Tribunals under the Act have the authority to order the eviction of a child or relative if it is deemed necessary for the protection of the senior citizen.
questions.solution:
Samtola Devi vs State of UP (2025)

In this ruling regarding the Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act, 2007, the Supreme Court clarified that while Tribunals have the authority to order eviction of children if necessary for a parent's protection, eviction is NOT mandatory in every case of neglect. The court must balance the circumstances of each family dispute.

questions.question: How does the writ jurisdiction of the High Courts differ from that of the Supreme Court?
questions.option_1: High Courts require prior approval from the Supreme Court before issuing writs.
questions.option_2: High Courts can issue writs only in appellate cases, not in original jurisdiction.
questions.option_3: High Courts can issue writs only against state governments, not the Centre.
questions.option_4: High Courts can issue writs for purposes beyond Fundamental Rights, unlike the Supreme Court.
questions.solution:
Writ Jurisdiction: SC vs HC

The writ jurisdiction of High Courts (Article 226) is wider than that of the Supreme Court (Article 32). While the Supreme Court can issue writs only for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights, High Courts can issue them for Fundamental Rights and for any other legal purpose.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Supreme Court’s writ jurisdiction is correct?
questions.option_1: It can only be invoked for enforcement of Fundamental Rights.
questions.option_2: It allows the Court to issue writs only against the Central Government.
questions.option_3: It extends to both Fundamental Rights and other legal rights.
questions.option_4: It is exclusive and cannot be exercised by the High Courts.
questions.solution:
Supreme Court’s Writ Jurisdiction

Under Article 32, the Supreme Court’s writ jurisdiction is specifically limited; it can only be invoked for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights. This makes Article 32 itself a Fundamental Right, often called the 'Heart and Soul' of the Constitution.

questions.question: Which type of subordinate court primarily handles disputes related to property ownership and contractual agreements?
questions.solution:
Civil Courts

In the hierarchy of subordinate courts, Civil Courts primarily handle disputes involving property ownership, contractual agreements, and monetary claims. They are distinct from Criminal Courts, which deal with offenses against the state like theft or assault.

questions.question: The Supreme Court can issue writs under Article 32 only for which specific purpose?
questions.option_3: To review decisions of statutory tribunals
questions.option_4: To enforce the Directive Principles of State Policy
questions.solution:
Article 32 and Fundamental Rights

Article 32 provides the right to move the Supreme Court by appropriate proceedings for the protection of Fundamental Rights. The court can issue directions or writs including Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Quo Warranto, and Certiorari.

questions.question: Which urban localself governmentbody manages small towns with limited municipal functions like street lighting and drainage?
questions.solution:
Town Area Committee

A Town Area Committee is a semi-municipal authority set up for the administration of a small town. It handles limited civic functions such as street lighting, drainage, and roads, and is usually governed by a separate act of the state legislature.

questions.question: Which of the following Urban Local Government Bodies in India was discontinued after 1992?
questions.solution:
Notified Area Committee

The Notified Area Committee was a body created for areas that did not yet fulfill the conditions for a municipality but were considered important. Following the 74th Amendment Act (1992), which standardized urban bodies into three categories (Nagar Panchayat, Municipal Council, Corporation), the Notified Area Committee was largely discontinued or merged into these new categories.

questions.question: Which among the following significant changes was introduced in the Waqf (Amendment) Act, 2025?
questions.option_1: Exemption of Waqf properties from legal scrutiny
questions.option_3: Mandating representation of at least two Muslim women on Waqf Boards
questions.option_4: Abolition of the Central Waqf Council
questions.solution:
Women’s Representation in Waqf

The Waqf (Amendment) Act, 2025, sought to modernize the administration of Waqf boards by ensuring mandatory representation of at least two Muslim women. This was intended to improve transparency and provide a more diverse perspective in the management of religious assets.

questions.question: In the ministerial hierarchy in India, Ministers of State generally have:
questions.option_1: no executive responsibility in any of the portfolios
questions.option_3: secondary rank within the portfolios assigned to Cabinet Ministers
questions.option_4: equal standing with Cabinet Ministers in their assigned portfolios
questions.solution:
Ministers of State

In the Indian ministerial hierarchy, Ministers of State hold a secondary rank. They are either given independent charge of a smaller ministry or are assigned to assist a Cabinet Minister in their larger portfolios, often looking after specific departments within that ministry.

questions.question: The Supreme Court described itself as the "sentinel on the qui vive" in relation to ____________.
questions.solution:
Sentinel on the Qui Vive

The Supreme Court described itself as the "sentinel on the qui vive" (a watchful guardian) specifically in relation to the protection of Fundamental Rights. It signifies the Court's proactive duty to ensure that the state does not infringe upon the basic liberties of citizens.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key reason for judicial review in India?
questions.option_1: To ensure the supremacy of the Parliament
questions.option_2: To protect the supremacy of the Constitution and fundamental rights
questions.option_3: To allow the judiciary to make laws
questions.option_4: To maintain federal balance between Centre and States
questions.solution:
Judicial Review in India

The key reason for Judicial Review is to protect the supremacy of the Constitution and safeguard Fundamental Rights. It allows the judiciary to examine the constitutionality of legislative enactments and executive orders, declaring them void if they violate the basic structure or the Constitution.

questions.question: In India, what are municipalities primarily responsible for?
questions.option_2: Administration of small cities or towns
questions.solution:
Role of Municipalities

In India, municipalities (Municipal Councils or Nagar Palikas) are primarily responsible for the administration of small cities or towns. Their duties include urban planning, water supply, public health, sanitation, and maintaining birth/death records.

questions.question: Which is NOT an advantage of a Lok Adalat?
questions.solution:
Lok Adalat and Appeals

Lok Adalats (People's Courts) are designed for speedy, informal dispute resolution. While their decisions are binding and have the status of a civil court decree, a key feature is that there is no right to appeal against the award of a Lok Adalat. If parties are dissatisfied, they must file a fresh suit in a regular court.

questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding the Supreme Court's interim order in the Ranveer Allahbadia case (February 2025) is/are correct? The Supreme Court granted interim protection to Allahbadia but harshly criticised his remarks as obscene and vulgar. The Court ruled that Allahbadia was free to travel abroad without any restrictions since the remarks were made behind a paywall.
questions.solution:
Ranveer Allahbadia Case (2025)

In February 2025, the Supreme Court granted interim protection to content creator Ranveer Allahbadia against coercive action. However, the Court criticised the nature of the remarks made in the controversial podcast episodes, highlighting that digital platforms must maintain standards of decency even behind paywalls.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is true about the Permanent Lok Adalat under Section 22-B of The Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987?
questions.option_1: The award of the Permanent Lok Adalat is not binding on the parties.
questions.option_2: The Permanent Lok Adalat has jurisdiction over disputes related to criminal offences.
questions.option_3: The Permanent Lok Adalat can handle cases up to a certain monetary limit (currently ₹1 crore).
questions.option_4: The Permanent Lok Adalat can only act as a conciliator and cannot decide disputes.
questions.solution:
Permanent Lok Adalat Jurisdiction

Under Section 22-B of the Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987, Permanent Lok Adalats (PLAs) handle public utility services (like water, power, or transport). They have the authority to decide a case on its merits if conciliation fails, and they currently handle disputes up to a monetary limit of ₹1 crore.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about Small Causes Courts is correct?
questions.option_1: They deal exclusively with criminal cases of a minor nature.
questions.option_2: Their decisions are final but subject to High Court’s revision.
questions.option_3: They can hear appeals from Nyaya Panchayats.
questions.option_4: They exist in all states under a uniform nomenclature.
questions.solution:
Small Causes Courts

Small Causes Courts deal with civil cases of small value. Their decisions are generally final, meaning there is no regular appeal against their judgment, although they are subject to the High Court’s revisional jurisdiction to ensure there was no failure of justice.

questions.question: Who among the following will be the 25ᵗʰ Chief Justice of India in May 2025?
questions.solution:
25th Chief Justice of India

Justice B.R. Gavai (Bhushan Ramkrishna Gavai) is scheduled to be the 25th Chief Justice of India, with his tenure expected to begin in May 2025. He will be the second Dalit CJI in India's history after Justice K.G. Balakrishnan.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a category of judicial review, as classified by Justice Syed Shah Mohammed Quadri?
questions.option_1: Judicial review of judicial appointments to the Supreme Court and High Courts
questions.option_2: Judicial review of administrative action of the Union and State and authorities under the state
questions.option_3: Judicial review of legislation of the Parliament and State Legislatures and subordinate legislations
questions.solution:
Categories of Judicial Review

Justice Syed Shah Mohammed Quadri classified judicial review into three main categories: (1) Constitutional Amendments, (2) Legislation (Parliamentary and State), and (3) Administrative Action. Judicial review of judicial appointments is a specific subset of legal debate but was not one of the three primary categories in his classic classification.
